examples.explanation: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3], rank = [0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots differ, union(0,1) -> parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0 (since parent[1]=0, find(0)=0), find(2)=2, roots differ, union(0,2) -> parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, roots differ, union(0,3) -> parent[3]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [3,0]: find(3)=0, find(0)=0, roots same, cycle detected -> return true.

examples.input: vertices: 3, edges: [[0,1], [1,2]]
examples.explanation: Initialize parent = [0,1,2], rank = [0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots differ, union(0,1) -> parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, roots differ, union(0,2) -> parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. All edges processed, no cycle -> return false.

approaches.name: Brute Force Union-Find with Repeated Edge Checks
approaches.summary: Iterate through all edges and use a union-find (disjoint set) data structure to detect cycles by checking if the two vertices of an edge are already in the same set before unioning them. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated edge processing and lack of graph traversal optimization.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function, but with repeated checks can degrade to O(E²) in naive implementations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph Using Brute Force Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection using union-find with repeated edge checks.
ladders.explanation: This approach detects cycles in an undirected graph by processing all edges and using a union-find (disjoint set) data structure. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, a cycle exists if we encounter an edge connecting two vertices that are already in the same connected component. We maintain a parent array where each vertex initially points to itself, representing separate sets. For each edge (u, v), we find the root (representative) of u and v using the find operation with path compression. If the roots are the same, u and v are already connected, indicating a cycle. Otherwise, we union the two sets by making one root point to the other, using union by rank to optimize. The brute force aspect involves processing all edges repeatedly without early termination, which can lead to inefficiencies. The algorithm iterates through the edge list, applying find and union operations. Time complexity is O(E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function, but without optimizations, it can degrade to O(E²) due to repeated processing. Space complexity is O(V) for the parent and rank arrays. This connects to L1 by relying on the union-find operations as sub-routines. Common mistakes include not handling self-loops correctly, forgetting to initialize parent arrays, or missing path compression in find. Real code pattern involves initializing arrays, iterating edges, and checking roots.

examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3], rank = [0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots differ, union(0,1) -> parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0 (since parent[1]=0, find(0)=0), find(2)=2, roots differ, union(0,2) -> parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=0, find(3)=3, roots differ, union(0,3) -> parent[3]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [3,0]: find(3)=0, find(0)=0, roots same, cycle detected -> return true.

examples.input: vertices: 3, edges: [[0,1], [1,2]]
examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2], rank = [0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots differ, union(0,1) -> parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0, find(2)=2, roots differ, union(0,2) -> parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1. All edges processed, no cycle -> return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Union-Find Operations with Path Compression and Union by Rank
ladders.desc: Implement find and union functions for disjoint sets.
ladders.explanation: The union-find data structure manages disjoint sets, crucial for cycle detection. The intuition is to group vertices into connected components, where each set has a representative root. The find operation determines the root of a vertex, using recursion and path compression to flatten the tree for efficiency. Path compression works by updating the parent of each visited node to point directly to the root during the find call, reducing future lookup times. The union operation merges two sets by attaching the root of one to the root of the other, using union by rank to keep the tree shallow. Rank is an estimate of height; when unioning, the root with lower rank is attached to the one with higher rank to minimize depth. This ensures near-constant time per operation. Time complexity is O(α(V)) per operation, where α is the inverse Ackermann function. Space complexity is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This feeds into L0 by providing the core functions to process edges. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression correctly, forgetting to update ranks, or using union without rank optimization. Real code pattern involves recursive find and conditional union based on ranks.

examples.input: parent = [0,0,1,2], rank = [1,0,0,0], operations: find(3), union(0,3)
examples.output: find(3)=0, union results in parent = [0,0,1,0], rank = [1,0,0,0]
examples.trace: find(3): parent[3]=2, find(2): parent[2]=1, find(1): parent[1]=0, find(0)=0, path compress: set parent[3]=0, parent[2]=0, parent[1]=0, return 0. union(0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=0, roots same, no union needed.

examples.input: parent = [0,1,2,3], rank = [0,0,0,0], operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2)
examples.trace: union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, ranks equal (0), attach 1 to 0, parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, ranks equal, attach 3 to 2, parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, rank[0]=1 > rank[2]=1? equal, attach 2 to 0, parent[2]=0, rank[0] unchanged.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Cycle by Checking Edge Connectivity in Union-Find
ladders.desc: The central technique of checking if two vertices are in the same set before unioning.
ladders.explanation: The core logic for cycle detection in an undirected graph using union-find revolves around the principle that a cycle exists if an edge connects two vertices that are already part of the same connected component. This is because in an undirected graph, adding an edge between vertices in the same component creates a redundant connection, forming a cycle. The algorithm processes each edge (u, v) sequentially. For each edge, it finds the root of u and the root of v using the find operation. If the roots are identical, it means u and v are already connected through some path, so adding this edge would create a cycle, and the algorithm returns true. If the roots are different, it means u and v are in disjoint sets, so it unions them to merge the sets, indicating they are now connected. This process continues until a cycle is detected or all edges are processed without finding one. The intuition is that union-find dynamically builds connected components, and any edge that falls within an existing component signals a cycle. Time complexity depends on the union-find operations, typically O(α(V)) per edge, leading to O(E * α(V)) overall. Space complexity is O(V) for the data structures. This feeds into L1 by utilizing the union-find operations to implement this check. Common mistakes include not handling self-loops (edges where u == v, which always indicate a cycle), processing edges in the wrong order, or missing early termination. Real code pattern involves a loop over edges with root comparisons and union calls.

examples.input: vertices: 3, edges: [[0,1], [1,2], [2,0]], process step-by-step
examples.trace: Start with disjoint sets: {0}, {1}, {2}. Edge [0,1]: roots 0 and 1 differ, union -> sets {0,1}, {2}. Edge [1,2]: root of 1 is 0, root of 2 is 2, differ, union -> sets {0,1,2}. Edge [2,0]: root of 2 is 0, root of 0 is 0, same, cycle detected.

examples.input: vertices: 4, edges: [[0,1], [2,3], [1,2]], process step-by-step
examples.trace: Start sets: {0}, {1}, {2}, {3}. Edge [0,1]: roots 0 and 1 differ, union -> {0,1}, {2}, {3}. Edge [2,3]: roots 2 and 3 differ, union -> {0,1}, {2,3}. Edge [1,2]: root of 1 is 0, root of 2 is 2, differ, union -> {0,1,2,3}. All edges processed, no cycle.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Edge Iteration
ladders.desc: Representing an undirected graph and iterating through its edges.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing an undirected graph in a way that facilitates cycle detection. The intuition is that a graph can be modeled as a collection of vertices and edges, where edges are unordered pairs (u, v) indicating a connection between vertices u and v. For cycle detection using union-find, we typically use an edge list representation, which is a list of pairs, each representing an edge. This is efficient for iterating through all edges sequentially, as required by the brute force approach. Alternatively, an adjacency list or matrix could be used, but edge list is straightforward for this method. The algorithm starts by initializing data structures based on the number of vertices, then iterates through each edge in the list. During iteration, for each edge (u, v), we perform operations like finding roots and unioning sets. It's important to handle undirected nature: edge (u, v) is the same as (v, u), so no direction is considered. Time complexity for iteration is O(E) for processing each edge once, contributing to the overall O(E * α(V)). Space complexity is O(E) for storing the edge list, plus O(V) for union-find structures. This feeds into L2 by providing the graph data to apply the core logic. Common mistakes include using directed graph representations, incorrect edge indexing, or not validating vertex ranges. Real code pattern involves defining an edge list and looping over it with indices or iterators.

examples.input: vertices: 3, edges: [[0,1], [1,2], [2,0]], iterate and print each edge
examples.trace: Initialize edge list = [[0,1], [1,2], [2,0]]. Iteration 1: edge = [0,1], print. Iteration 2: edge = [1,2], print. Iteration 3: edge = [2,0], print.

examples.input: vertices: 4, edges: [[0,1], [2,3]], iterate and check if u < v
examples.trace: Edge list = [[0,1], [2,3]]. Iteration 1: u=0, v=1, 0<1 true. Iteration 2: u=2, v=3, 2<3 true.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Initializing and Updating Arrays for Disjoint Sets
ladders.desc: Setting up parent and rank arrays for union-find.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves initializing and maintaining the data structures for the union-find algorithm. The intuition is that we need to represent disjoint sets, where each set has a representative element (root). We use two arrays: parent and rank. The parent array stores the parent of each vertex; initially, each vertex is its own parent, indicating it is in a separate set. The rank array stores an estimate of the depth of the tree rooted at each vertex; initially, all ranks are 0, as each vertex is a singleton tree. During operations, we update these arrays: in find, we may update parent pointers for path compression; in union, we update parent and rank based on the union-by-rank heuristic. Initialization is done by looping through all vertices and setting parent[i] = i and rank[i] = 0. This ensures a clean start for the union-find process. The operation is fundamental because without proper initialization, the algorithm may fail to correctly track connected components. Time complexity for initialization is O(V), as we iterate through all vertices. Space complexity is O(V) for the arrays. This feeds into L3 by providing the initialized structures for graph processing. Common mistakes include not allocating enough space for the arrays, using incorrect initial values, or not updating ranks during union. Real code pattern involves simple loops to set initial values.

examples.input: vertices: 4, initialize parent and rank
examples.trace: For i from 0 to 3: set parent[i] = i, so parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2, parent[3]=3. Set rank[i] = 0 for all i.

examples.input: vertices: 5, update after union(0,1): parent = [0,0,2,3,4], rank = [1,0,0,0,0]
examples.trace: Initial: parent = [0,1,2,3,4], rank = [0,0,0,0,0]. After union(0,1): find roots, attach 1 to 0, set parent[1]=0, increment rank[0] to 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sets and Connectivity in Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic idea of connected components and disjoint sets.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the idea of sets and connectivity in graphs, which is essential for understanding union-find. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, vertices can be grouped into connected components, where each component is a set of vertices that are reachable from each other via edges. Disjoint sets are sets that have no elements in common; initially, each vertex is in its own set. As edges are added, sets are merged to reflect connectivity. For example, if vertices 0 and 1 are connected by an edge, they move from separate sets {0} and {1} to a combined set {0,1}. This concept is visual: imagine vertices as dots and edges as lines; connected components are clusters of dots connected by lines. The union-find data structure manages these sets efficiently. Understanding this helps in cycle detection: if an edge tries to connect two vertices already in the same set, it forms a cycle because there's already a path between them. This is a beginner-friendly concept, focusing on visualization rather than algorithms. Time and space are not analyzed here, as it's conceptual. This feeds into L4 by providing the theoretical basis for initializing sets. Common mistakes include confusing directed and undirected connectivity, or not grasping that sets represent equivalence relations. Real code pattern is not applicable at this level; instead, use diagrams or examples.

examples.input: Vertices: 0,1,2. Edges: [0,1]. Describe sets before and after adding edge.
examples.trace: Initially, each vertex is alone: set for 0, set for 1, set for 2. Add edge between 0 and 1: now 0 and 1 are connected, so merge their sets into one. Vertex 2 remains separate.

examples.input: Vertices: 0,1,2,3. Edges: [0,1], [2,3]. What are the connected components?
examples.trace: Edge [0,1] connects 0 and 1 into one component. Edge [2,3] connects 2 and 3 into another component. No edges between these groups, so two separate components.

testCases.expected: Sets: {0}, {1}, {2} (self-loop doesn't change sets for connectivity)

approaches.name: DFS with Parent Tracking
approaches.summary: Perform a depth-first search (DFS) traversal of the graph, keeping track of visited nodes and the parent node to avoid mistaking the immediate back edge as a cycle. If a visited node is encountered that is not the parent, a cycle is detected. This is the standard approach for undirected graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph Using DFS with Parent Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to detect cycles in an undirected graph using DFS with parent tracking.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires determining whether an undirected graph contains a cycle. The DFS with parent tracking approach works by performing a depth-first traversal while maintaining two key pieces of information: visited nodes and the parent node for each node during traversal. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, during DFS, if we encounter a visited node that is not the immediate parent of the current node, we've found a back edge indicating a cycle. This works because in undirected graphs, edges are bidirectional, so the only legitimate visited neighbor should be the parent (where we just came from). Any other visited neighbor means we've reached a node through a different path, creating a cycle.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Create a visited array/map to track visited nodes.
2. For each unvisited node in the graph:
   a. Start DFS from that node with parent = -1 (no parent).
   b. Mark current node as visited.
   c. For each neighbor of current node:
      i. If neighbor is not visited, recursively call DFS with current node as parent.
      ii. If neighbor is visited AND neighbor ≠ parent, return true (cycle detected).
   d. If no cycle found in this component, continue to next unvisited node.
3. If all nodes processed without cycle detection, return false.

Time complexity: O(V + E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as we visit each node and edge once.
Space complexity: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack.

Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the DFS traversal subroutine with cycle detection logic.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle disconnected graphs (need to check all components).
2. Not tracking parent correctly, leading to false positives (mistaking parent-child edge as cycle).
3. Using directed graph cycle detection logic which doesn't work for undirected graphs.

Real code pattern:
def has_cycle(graph):
    visited = [False] * len(graph)
    for node in range(len(graph)):
        if not visited[node]:
            if dfs(node, -1, visited, graph):
                return True
    return False

def dfs(current, parent, visited, graph):
    visited[current] = True
    for neighbor in graph[current]:
        if not visited[neighbor]:
            if dfs(neighbor, current, visited, graph):
                return True
        elif neighbor != parent:
            return True
    return False

examples.input: Graph with 4 nodes: 0-1, 1-2, 2-3, 3-0 (cycle present)
examples.trace: Start at node 0 (unvisited). DFS(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1,3. Check neighbor 1 (unvisited): DFS(1, 0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0 (visited but 0 = parent, so ignore). Check neighbor 2 (unvisited): DFS(2, 1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1,3. Check neighbor 1 (visited but 1 = parent, ignore). Check neighbor 3 (unvisited): DFS(3, 2): Mark 3 visited. Neighbors: 2,0. Check neighbor 2 (visited but 2 = parent, ignore). Check neighbor 0 (visited AND 0 ≠ parent 2) → CYCLE DETECTED. Return True up the chain.

examples.input: Graph with 4 nodes: 0-1, 1-2, 2-3 (no cycle, linear chain)
examples.trace: Start at node 0 (unvisited). DFS(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1 (unvisited): DFS(1, 0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0 (visited but 0 = parent, ignore). Check neighbor 2 (unvisited): DFS(2, 1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1,3. Check neighbor 1 (visited but 1 = parent, ignore). Check neighbor 3 (unvisited): DFS(3, 2): Mark 3 visited. Neighbors: 2. Check neighbor 2 (visited but 2 = parent, ignore). No more neighbors, return False. Back to DFS(2): All neighbors processed, return False. Back to DFS(1): All neighbors processed, return False. Back to DFS(0): All neighbors processed, return False. All nodes visited, no cycle detected.

testCases.input: Graph: Single node 0 with no edges

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Parent Parameter for Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: The recursive DFS function that traverses the graph and detects cycles by checking visited neighbors against parent.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is the heart of the cycle detection algorithm. It performs a depth-first traversal from a given starting node while tracking the parent node to distinguish between legitimate back edges (to parent) and cycle-indicating back edges (to non-parent visited nodes). The intuition is that during DFS in an undirected graph, each edge is traversed twice (once in each direction). The parent parameter tells us which direction we came from, so when we encounter a visited neighbor, we check: if it's the parent, this is just the edge we came from (normal); if it's not the parent, we've found a different path to this node, indicating a cycle.

Algorithm:
1. Mark current node as visited.
2. For each neighbor of current node:
   a. If neighbor is not visited: recursively call DFS with current as parent.
   b. If DFS returns true (cycle found), propagate true upward.
   c. If neighbor is visited AND neighbor ≠ parent: return true immediately (cycle detected).
3. If all neighbors processed without cycle detection, return false.

Time complexity: O(V + E) for the component being traversed.
Space complexity: O(V) for recursion stack.

Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the main solution to check each connected component.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to pass the parent parameter correctly in recursive calls.
2. Not handling the base case where parent = -1 for the starting node.
3. Incorrectly checking 'neighbor != parent' - must be strict inequality.

Real code pattern:
def dfs(current, parent, visited, graph):
    visited[current] = True
    for neighbor in graph[current]:
        if not visited[neighbor]:
            if dfs(neighbor, current, visited, graph):
                return True
        elif neighbor != parent:
            return True
    return False

examples.input: Start DFS at node 0, parent=-1, graph: 0-1, 1-2, 2-0
examples.trace: DFS(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1 (unvisited): DFS(1, 0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0 (visited AND 0 ≠ parent 0? Wait, parent is 0, neighbor is 0, so neighbor = parent → ignore). Check neighbor 2 (unvisited): DFS(2, 1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1,0. Check neighbor 1 (visited but 1 = parent, ignore). Check neighbor 0 (visited AND 0 ≠ parent 1) → CYCLE DETECTED. Return True.

examples.input: Start DFS at node 0, parent=-1, graph: 0-1, 1-2
examples.trace: DFS(0, -1): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1 (unvisited): DFS(1, 0): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: 0,2. Check neighbor 0 (visited but 0 = parent, ignore). Check neighbor 2 (unvisited): DFS(2, 1): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: 1. Check neighbor 1 (visited but 1 = parent, ignore). No more neighbors, return False. Back to DFS(1): All neighbors processed, return False. Back to DFS(0): All neighbors processed, return False.

testCases.input: Start node: 0, parent: -1, graph: Single node 0
Start node: 0, parent: -1, graph: 0-1, 1-0 (just two nodes connected)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Distinguishing Parent Back Edge from Cycle Back Edge in Undirected DFS
ladders.desc: The central insight: In undirected graph DFS, a visited neighbor indicates a cycle only if it's not the immediate parent.
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental insight that makes cycle detection in undirected graphs different from directed graphs. In an undirected graph, edges have no direction, so when doing DFS, each edge will be encountered twice: once going from node A to B, and once from B to A. The parent tracking mechanism helps us distinguish between these two encounters. When we reach a visited node during DFS, we must check: is this node the one we just came from (the parent)? If yes, this is just the reverse direction of the edge we're currently traversing - this is normal and doesn't indicate a cycle. If no, this means we've reached a node that was already visited via a different path, which creates a cycle.

Why this works:
1. In a tree (acyclic undirected graph), DFS will only encounter each edge twice (forward and backward).
2. In a cyclic graph, there exists at least one edge that connects two nodes that are already connected by another path.
3. When DFS follows this edge, it will encounter a visited node that isn't the immediate parent.

Step-by-step logic:
1. During DFS traversal, for each neighbor of current node:
2. If neighbor is visited: check if neighbor == parent
   a. If equal: this is the edge we came from (ignore)
   b. If not equal: cycle detected!
3. If neighbor is not visited: continue DFS recursively.

Time complexity: O(1) per edge check.
Space complexity: O(1) for the comparison.

Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the DFS subroutine's neighbor checking condition.

Common mistakes:
1. Confusing this with directed graph cycle detection (which uses recursion stack or colors).
2. Not understanding why parent comparison is necessary.
3. Implementing this check incorrectly in the recursion base case.

Real code pattern:
for neighbor in graph[current]:
    if not visited[neighbor]:
        # recursive call
    elif neighbor != parent:
        return True  # Cycle detected!

examples.input: Current node: 2, parent: 1, visited: {0,1,2}, neighbors: [1,3], graph: 0-1-2-3
examples.trace: Processing node 2 with parent 1. Neighbors: 1 and 3. Check neighbor 1: visited = True. Is neighbor (1) ≠ parent (1)? No, they're equal. This is the edge we came from (2-1). Ignore. Check neighbor 3: visited = False, so we'll explore it recursively. No cycle detected from this check.

examples.input: Current node: 3, parent: 2, visited: {0,1,2,3}, neighbors: [2,0], graph: 0-1-2-3-0 (cycle)
examples.output: Cycle detected (neighbor 0 is visited and not parent)
examples.trace: Processing node 3 with parent 2. Neighbors: 2 and 0. Check neighbor 2: visited = True. Is neighbor (2) ≠ parent (2)? No, equal. Ignore. Check neighbor 0: visited = True. Is neighbor (0) ≠ parent (2)? Yes! 0 ≠ 2. This means we've found a back edge to node 0 which is not our parent. Cycle detected!

testCases.input: Current: 1, parent: 0, visited neighbors: [0,2], check neighbor 0
Current: 2, parent: 1, visited neighbors: [1,3], check neighbor 3 (unvisited)
Current: 3, parent: 2, visited neighbors: [2,0], check neighbor 0 (visited, parent=2)
Current: 0, parent: -1, visited neighbors: [1], check neighbor 1 (unvisited)
Current: 1, parent: 0, visited neighbors: [0,2], check neighbor 2 (unvisited)
Current: 2, parent: 1, visited neighbors: [1,0], check neighbor 0 (visited, parent=1)
Current: 0, parent: -1, visited neighbors: [1,2], check neighbor 1 (unvisited)
Current: 1, parent: 0, visited neighbors: [0,2], check neighbor 0 (visited, parent=0)
Current: 2, parent: 1, visited neighbors: [1,3,4], check neighbor 4 (visited, parent=1)
Current: 3, parent: 2, visited neighbors: [2], check neighbor 2 (visited, parent=2)

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Adjacency List Traversal
ladders.desc: Representing an undirected graph as an adjacency list and iterating through neighbors.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on how to represent an undirected graph in code and how to traverse it. The adjacency list representation is most suitable for DFS-based algorithms because it allows efficient iteration of a node's neighbors. In an undirected graph, each edge (u,v) appears twice in the adjacency list: once in u's list and once in v's list. This bidirectional representation is crucial for the parent tracking approach to work correctly.

Intuition: We need a data structure that lets us quickly access all neighbors of a given node. An adjacency list provides O(degree) time to iterate through neighbors, which is optimal for DFS. The undirected nature means we add each edge to both nodes' lists.

Step-by-step:
1. Create an array of lists/vectors of size V (number of vertices).
2. For each edge (u,v):
   a. Add v to adjacency list of u
   b. Add u to adjacency list of v
3. To traverse: for a given node, iterate through its adjacency list to access all neighbors.

Time complexity: O(V + E) to build, O(degree) to access neighbors of a node.
Space complexity: O(V + E) to store the adjacency lists.

Connection to L2: This representation enables the neighbor iteration where we apply the parent-checking logic.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to add edges in both directions for undirected graphs.
2. Using adjacency matrix which wastes space for sparse graphs.
3. Not initializing the adjacency list properly (empty lists for each node).

Real code pattern:
# Build adjacency list
adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]
for u,v in edges:
    adj[u].append(v)
    adj[v].append(u)

# Traverse neighbors of node u
for neighbor in adj[u]:
    # process neighbor

examples.input: Graph with 3 nodes, edges: 0-1, 1-2
examples.trace: Initialize adj list for 3 nodes: [[], [], []]. Process edge 0-1: Add 1 to adj[0] → [[1], [], []]. Add 0 to adj[1] → [[1], [0], []]. Process edge 1-2: Add 2 to adj[1] → [[1], [0,2], []]. Add 1 to adj[2] → [[1], [0,2], [1]]. Final: [[1], [0,2], [1]].

examples.input: Graph with 4 nodes, edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-2, 2-3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive DFS Traversal with Visited Tracking
ladders.desc: Perform depth-first search on a graph using recursion while marking visited nodes.
ladders.explanation:
This operation is the fundamental graph traversal technique. Depth-first search (DFS) explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For graphs, we use a visited set/array to avoid revisiting nodes and prevent infinite loops. The recursive implementation is natural for DFS because it uses the call stack to track the path. The intuition is to 'go deep' first: from a starting node, visit one neighbor, then from that neighbor visit its unvisited neighbor, and so on, until we reach a dead end, then backtrack.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Mark current node as visited.
2. For each neighbor of current node:
   a. If neighbor is not visited:
      i. Recursively call DFS on neighbor.
3. The recursion naturally backtracks when all neighbors are visited or processed.

Time complexity: O(V + E) for the connected component.
Space complexity: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack depth.

Connection to L3: This traversal operates on the adjacency list representation to visit nodes.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to mark nodes as visited before recursion (causes infinite recursion).
2. Not handling disconnected graphs (need to call DFS on each unvisited node).
3. Using BFS queue instead of recursion stack.

Real code pattern:
def dfs(node, visited, graph):
    visited[node] = True
    for neighbor in graph[node]:
        if not visited[neighbor]:
            dfs(neighbor, visited, graph)

examples.input: Start at node 0, graph: 0-1, 1-2, 2-3
examples.trace: DFS(0): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: [1]. 1 not visited → DFS(1): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: [0,2]. 0 visited (skip), 2 not visited → DFS(2): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: [1,3]. 1 visited (skip), 3 not visited → DFS(3): Mark 3 visited. Neighbors: [2]. 2 visited (skip). Backtrack to DFS(2): All neighbors processed. Backtrack to DFS(1): All neighbors processed. Backtrack to DFS(0): All neighbors processed. Visited: {0,1,2,3}.

examples.input: Start at node 0, graph: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 2-4
examples.trace: DFS(0): Mark 0 visited. Neighbors: [1,2]. Process 1 first (order depends on implementation): 1 not visited → DFS(1): Mark 1 visited. Neighbors: [0,3]. 0 visited (skip), 3 not visited → DFS(3): Mark 3 visited. Neighbors: [1]. 1 visited (skip). Backtrack to DFS(1): All neighbors processed. Backtrack to DFS(0): Next neighbor 2: 2 not visited → DFS(2): Mark 2 visited. Neighbors: [0,4]. 0 visited (skip), 4 not visited → DFS(4): Mark 4 visited. Neighbors: [2]. 2 visited (skip). Backtrack to DFS(2): All neighbors processed. Backtrack to DFS(0): All neighbors processed. Visited: {0,1,3,2,4}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Basics: Nodes, Edges, and Connected Components
ladders.desc: Understanding the fundamental concepts of undirected graphs: vertices, edges, and connected components.
ladders.explanation:
This is the absolute foundation for graph algorithms. A graph is a collection of nodes (vertices) connected by edges. In an undirected graph, edges have no direction - the connection between node A and node B is bidirectional. A connected component is a subset of nodes where there's a path between any two nodes in that subset. Understanding these concepts is crucial because cycle detection depends on traversing these connections.

Key concepts:
1. Node/Vertex: A fundamental unit of the graph (usually represented by integers 0 to V-1).
2. Edge: A connection between two nodes. In undirected graphs, edge (u,v) is the same as (v,u).
3. Connected Component: A maximal set of nodes where every pair is connected by some path.
4. Path: A sequence of nodes where consecutive nodes are connected by edges.
5. Cycle: A path that starts and ends at the same node with no repetitions of other nodes.

Why this matters for cycle detection: We need to check each connected component separately because a cycle in one component doesn't affect other components. The DFS algorithm will naturally explore one component at a time.

Visual intuition: Think of nodes as dots and edges as lines connecting them. A cycle looks like a closed loop. In a tree (graph without cycles), there's exactly one path between any two nodes. Add one edge to a tree, and you create a cycle.

Common mistakes:
1. Confusing directed and undirected graphs.
2. Not understanding that graphs can have multiple disconnected components.
3. Thinking that all nodes must be connected in a graph.

Real code pattern:
# Basic graph concepts (no code, just understanding)
# V = number of vertices/nodes
# E = number of edges
# edges = list of pairs (u,v) representing connections

examples.input: Graph with 5 nodes: edges: 0-1, 1-2, 3-4
examples.trace: Nodes: 0,1,2,3,4. Edges: 0-1 connects 0 and 1. Edge 1-2 connects 1 and 2 (so 0,1,2 are all connected). Edge 3-4 connects 3 and 4 separately. So we have two groups: Component 1: nodes 0,1,2 (path exists between any two: e.g., 0→1→2). Component 2: nodes 3,4 (connected directly). No connection between components.

examples.input: Graph with 4 nodes: edges: 0-1, 1-2, 2-3, 3-0
examples.output: One connected component with a cycle
examples.trace: All 4 nodes are connected in a cycle: 0-1-2-3-0. From any node, you can reach any other node: 0→1→2→3, or 0→3→2→1, etc. This forms a single connected component. The cycle is evident: starting at 0, follow 0→1→2→3→0 returns to start.

approaches.name: BFS with Parent Tracking
approaches.summary: Use breadth-first search (BFS) to traverse the graph level by level, tracking visited nodes and parent nodes to detect cycles when a visited node is encountered that is not the parent. This approach explores the graph in a different order than DFS but achieves the same goal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph using BFS with Parent Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection in undirected graphs using BFS traversal with parent node tracking.
ladders.explanation: This solution detects cycles in undirected graphs by performing BFS traversal while tracking parent nodes. The intuition is that in an undirected graph, a cycle exists if during BFS traversal, we encounter a visited node that is not the parent of the current node. This happens because BFS explores level by level, and if we find a node already visited that isn't our immediate predecessor, it means there's an alternative path to that node, forming a cycle. The algorithm works by iterating through all vertices (to handle disconnected graphs), performing BFS from unvisited vertices. During BFS, we maintain a queue of (node, parent) pairs. For each neighbor, if it's not visited, we mark it visited, set its parent, and enqueue it. If it's visited and not the parent, we've found a cycle. Time complexity is O(V + E) since we visit each vertex and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array, parent array, and queue. This connects to the broader graph theory concept of cycle detection, where BFS provides an alternative to DFS with similar guarantees but different exploration order. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle disconnected graphs (need to iterate all vertices), incorrectly handling self-loops (which are cycles in undirected graphs), and confusing directed vs undirected graph logic (in undirected, edge (u,v) means both u→v and v→u exist). The parent tracking is crucial because in undirected graphs, we naturally encounter the edge back to the parent, which shouldn't be mistaken for a cycle.

examples.input: Graph with 4 vertices: edges = [[0,1], [1,2], [2,3], [3,0]]
examples.trace: Step 1: Start BFS from vertex 0 (unvisited). Queue: [(0, -1)], Visited: {0}, Parent: {0:-1}. Step 2: Process (0, -1). Neighbors: 1,3. Neighbor 1: not visited → enqueue (1,0), Visited: {0,1}, Parent: {0:-1,1:0}. Neighbor 3: not visited → enqueue (3,0), Visited: {0,1,3}, Parent: {0:-1,1:0,3:0}. Queue: [(1,0),(3,0)]. Step 3: Process (1,0). Neighbors: 0,2. Neighbor 0: visited but is parent (0) → ignore. Neighbor 2: not visited → enqueue (2,1), Visited: {0,1,2,3}, Parent: {0:-1,1:0,2:1,3:0}. Queue: [(3,0),(2,1)]. Step 4: Process (3,0). Neighbors: 2,0. Neighbor 2: visited and not parent (parent of 3 is 0, but 2's parent is 1) → CYCLE DETECTED. Return True.

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices: edges = [[0,1], [1,2]]
examples.output: False (no cycle, it's a simple path)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start BFS from vertex 0. Queue: [(0,-1)], Visited: {0}, Parent: {0:-1}. Step 2: Process (0,-1). Neighbor 1: not visited → enqueue (1,0), Visited: {0,1}, Parent: {0:-1,1:0}. Queue: [(1,0)]. Step 3: Process (1,0). Neighbors: 0,2. Neighbor 0: visited but is parent → ignore. Neighbor 2: not visited → enqueue (2,1), Visited: {0,1,2}, Parent: {0:-1,1:0,2:1}. Queue: [(2,1)]. Step 4: Process (2,1). Neighbor 1: visited but is parent → ignore. Queue empty. Step 5: All vertices visited, no cycle found. Return False.

testCases.expected: False (same edge listed twice, still no cycle)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Parent Tracking for a Connected Component
ladders.desc: Implement BFS traversal that tracks parent nodes and detects cycles within a connected component.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs BFS traversal on a connected component of an undirected graph while tracking parent relationships to detect cycles. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level, and by remembering each node's parent (the node we came from), we can distinguish between the natural back-edge to the parent (which isn't a cycle) and edges to other visited nodes (which indicate a cycle). The algorithm starts from a source node, uses a queue to process nodes, and for each node, examines all neighbors. If a neighbor is unvisited, we mark it visited, record its parent, and enqueue it. If a neighbor is visited and is not the parent, we've found a cycle. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(V_c + E_c) where V_c and E_c are vertices and edges in the connected component. Space complexity is O(V_c) for the queue and parent tracking. This feeds into the full problem by being the core traversal mechanism that gets called for each connected component. Common mistakes include: not initializing parent correctly for the source node (typically -1 or None), forgetting that in undirected graphs each edge appears twice in adjacency lists (need to handle gracefully), and incorrectly returning early without completing BFS (should only return early if cycle found). The parent tracking is what enables cycle detection without needing additional data structures like recursion stacks.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0 in graph: edges = [[0,1], [1,2], [2,0]]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue with (0, -1). Visited: {0}, Parent: {0:-1}. Step 2: Process (0,-1). Neighbors: 1,2. Neighbor 1: not visited → enqueue (1,0), Visited: {0,1}, Parent: {1:0}. Neighbor 2: not visited → enqueue (2,0), Visited: {0,1,2}, Parent: {2:0}. Queue: [(1,0),(2,0)]. Step 3: Process (1,0). Neighbors: 0,2. Neighbor 0: visited but is parent → ignore. Neighbor 2: visited and not parent (parent of 1 is 0, but 2's parent is 0) → CYCLE DETECTED. Return True.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0 in graph: edges = [[0,1], [1,2]]
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [(0,-1)], Visited: {0}, Parent: {0:-1}. Step 2: Process (0,-1). Neighbor 1: not visited → enqueue (1,0), Visited: {0,1}, Parent: {1:0}. Queue: [(1,0)]. Step 3: Process (1,0). Neighbors: 0,2. Neighbor 0: visited but is parent → ignore. Neighbor 2: not visited → enqueue (2,1), Visited: {0,1,2}, Parent: {2:1}. Queue: [(2,1)]. Step 4: Process (2,1). Neighbor 1: visited but is parent → ignore. Queue empty. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Cycle When Encountering Visited Non-Parent Node
ladders.desc: The central condition that identifies a cycle: when BFS encounters a visited node that isn't the current node's parent.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the heart of cycle detection in undirected graphs using BFS. The intuition is that in a tree (acyclic undirected graph), each node has exactly one parent in the BFS tree. If during BFS we encounter a neighbor that has already been visited and that neighbor is not our parent, it means there exists an alternative path to reach that neighbor, creating a cycle. This works because BFS explores all possible paths level by level, and if we find a node already in the visited set that isn't where we just came from, it must have been reached via a different route, indicating a cycle. The condition is simple: for current node u with parent p, when examining neighbor v, if v is visited and v ≠ p, return true (cycle detected). This logic relies on the graph being undirected—in directed graphs, the condition would be different. Time complexity for this check is O(1) per edge. Space complexity is O(1) additional beyond visited/parent tracking. This feeds into the BFS sub-routine by being the critical decision point during neighbor examination. Common mistakes include: confusing parent with visited status (need both pieces of information), not handling the initial node's parent correctly (often set to -1 or None), and incorrectly applying this to directed graphs. The beauty of this logic is its simplicity—it transforms cycle detection from a complex problem into a simple condition check during standard graph traversal.

examples.input: Current node u=1 with parent p=0, examining neighbor v=2 where visited[2]=True and parent[2]=0
examples.output: Cycle detected (v is visited and v ≠ p)
examples.trace: Step 1: Check if v is visited: visited[2] = True. Step 2: Check if v equals parent p: v=2, p=0 → 2 ≠ 0. Step 3: Since v is visited and v ≠ p, condition satisfied → cycle exists. This means node 2 was reached through a different path than from node 1, creating a cycle.

examples.input: Current node u=2 with parent p=1, examining neighbor v=1 where visited[1]=True and parent[1]=0
examples.output: No cycle (v is visited but v = p)
examples.trace: Step 1: Check if v is visited: visited[1] = True. Step 2: Check if v equals parent p: v=1, p=1 → 1 = 1. Step 3: Since v = p, condition not satisfied → no cycle. This is the expected back-edge to the parent in an undirected graph.

testCases.expected: Cycle (self-loop, v visited and v≠p since p=-1)

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS Traversal of a Graph
ladders.desc: Implement standard breadth-first search traversal for an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: BFS traversal explores a graph level by level, starting from a source node. The intuition is to visit all neighbors of the current node before moving to nodes further away, using a queue to maintain the order of exploration. This systematic approach ensures we visit nodes in increasing order of distance from the source. The algorithm: 1) Initialize a queue with the source node. 2) Mark source as visited. 3) While queue is not empty: dequeue a node, process it, then enqueue all its unvisited neighbors and mark them visited. For undirected graphs, we typically use an adjacency list representation. Time complexity is O(V + E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue and visited set. This feeds into cycle detection by providing the traversal framework—the parent tracking and cycle condition are added to this basic BFS. Common mistakes include: not marking nodes as visited before enqueuing (causing duplicates), forgetting to handle disconnected graphs (need outer loop), and using stack instead of queue (which would make it DFS). BFS is fundamental because it guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs and explores systematically, making it ideal for cycle detection when augmented with parent information.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0 in graph: edges = [[0,1], [0,2], [1,3], [2,3]]
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [0], Visited: {0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0. Process 0. Neighbors: 1,2. Enqueue 1 and 2, Visited: {0,1,2}. Queue: [1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 1. Process 1. Neighbors: 0,3. 0 visited → skip. 3 not visited → enqueue 3, Visited: {0,1,2,3}. Queue: [2,3]. Step 4: Dequeue 2. Process 2. Neighbors: 0,3. Both visited → skip. Queue: [3]. Step 5: Dequeue 3. Process 3. Neighbors: 1,2 → both visited. Queue empty. Traversal complete.

examples.input: Start from vertex 0 in graph: edges = [[0,1], [1,2]]
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [0], Visited: {0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0. Process 0. Neighbor 1: enqueue 1, Visited: {0,1}. Queue: [1]. Step 3: Dequeue 1. Process 1. Neighbors: 0,2. 0 visited → skip. 2 not visited → enqueue 2, Visited: {0,1,2}. Queue: [2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2. Process 2. Neighbor 1 visited → skip. Queue empty.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Operations for BFS
ladders.desc: Implement and use queue data structure operations (enqueue, dequeue) for BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) data structure essential for BFS traversal. The intuition is that BFS needs to process nodes in the order they're discovered: the first node discovered should be the first processed, which matches queue behavior. Basic operations: enqueue (add to back), dequeue (remove from front), peek (view front without removing), and isEmpty check. In BFS, we enqueue nodes when we discover them and dequeue to process them. This ensures level-order traversal—all nodes at distance k are processed before nodes at distance k+1. Time complexity for queue operations is typically O(1) for array/list implementations. Space complexity is O(n) where n is maximum queue size. This feeds into BFS traversal as the core mechanism for managing the frontier of nodes to explore. Common mistakes include: using list with pop(0) in Python (O(n) time—should use collections.deque), confusing queue with stack (LIFO), and not checking isEmpty before dequeueing. The queue is what distinguishes BFS from DFS (which uses a stack). Understanding queues is fundamental because they appear in many algorithms beyond BFS, including task scheduling and buffer management.

examples.input: Enqueue: 0,1,2 then dequeue twice
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty queue: []. Step 2: Enqueue 0: queue = [0]. Step 3: Enqueue 1: queue = [0,1]. Step 4: Enqueue 2: queue = [0,1,2]. Step 5: Dequeue: remove front (0), return 0, queue = [1,2]. Step 6: Dequeue: remove front (1), return 1, queue = [2].

examples.input: Enqueue: 5,3,7 then dequeue all until empty
examples.trace: Step 1: Empty queue: []. Step 2: Enqueue 5: [5]. Step 3: Enqueue 3: [5,3]. Step 4: Enqueue 7: [5,3,7]. Step 5: Dequeue: remove 5, return 5, queue = [3,7]. Step 6: Dequeue: remove 3, return 3, queue = [7]. Step 7: Dequeue: remove 7, return 7, queue = []. Step 8: isEmpty check: true.

testCases.expected: Error or None on second dequeue

testCases.input: Enqueue: 9, Dequeue, Enqueue: 8, Dequeue, Enqueue: 7, Dequeue
Enqueue: 4,5,6, Dequeue, Enqueue: 7, Dequeue, Dequeue, Dequeue

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation using Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent an undirected graph using adjacency list data structure.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list represents a graph as an array of lists, where each index corresponds to a vertex, and the list at that index contains all vertices adjacent to it. For undirected graphs, if there's an edge between u and v, v appears in u's list and u appears in v's list. The intuition is that this representation efficiently stores sparse graphs (where E is much less than V²) and allows quick access to a vertex's neighbors. To build it: initialize an array of empty lists of size V, then for each edge (u,v), append v to u's list and u to v's list. Time complexity to build is O(V + E). Space complexity is O(V + E). This is the foundation for graph traversal algorithms like BFS and DFS because they need to efficiently access neighbors. Common mistakes include: not initializing all lists, forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs, and using incorrect data structures (like matrix for sparse graphs wasting space). Understanding adjacency lists is crucial because most graph algorithms assume this representation, and it's more space-efficient than adjacency matrices for typical problems.

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices, edges = [[0,1], [1,2]]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize list of size 3: [[], [], []]. Step 2: Process edge (0,1): append 1 to list[0] → [[1], [], []]. Append 0 to list[1] → [[1], [0], []]. Step 3: Process edge (1,2): append 2 to list[1] → [[1], [0,2], []]. Append 1 to list[2] → [[1], [0,2], [1]]. Final: [[1], [0,2], [1]].

examples.input: Graph with 4 vertices, edges = [[0,1], [0,2], [1,3], [2,3]]

approaches.name: Optimized Union-Find with Path Compression
approaches.summary: Apply union-find with path compression and union by rank to efficiently process edges in a single pass, detecting a cycle if two vertices of an edge are in the same set. This method avoids graph traversal and is highly efficient for dynamic connectivity queries.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function, nearly constant

ladders.title: Full Problem — Detect Cycle in Undirected Graph Using Optimized Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of cycle detection using union-find with path compression and union by rank.
ladders.explanation: This approach detects cycles in an undirected graph by processing edges one by one using the union-find data structure. The intuition is that a cycle exists if we encounter an edge connecting two vertices that are already in the same connected component. Union-find efficiently tracks connectivity through parent pointers and ranks. For each edge (u, v), we find the roots of u and v. If they are the same, a cycle is detected because both vertices are already connected through some path. Otherwise, we union them by attaching the root of the lower-rank tree to the higher-rank root, with path compression flattening parent chains during finds to optimize future operations. This method avoids explicit graph traversal and works in near-linear time due to the inverse Ackermann function's slow growth. The algorithm initializes parent arrays where each node is its own parent and rank arrays with zeros. It iterates through edges, performing find operations with path compression (recursively updating parents to point directly to the root) and union by rank (attaching smaller trees to larger ones to keep trees shallow). A cycle is reported as soon as two vertices of an edge share the same root. Time complexity is O(E * α(V)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function (practically constant), and space is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the union operation from L1 with initialization and edge processing. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize ranks, not compressing paths fully, or incorrectly handling self-loops (which are cycles in undirected graphs). Real code pattern: Initialize parent[i] = i, rank[i] = 0; for each edge (u, v): rootU = find(u), rootV = find(v); if rootU == rootV: return True; else: union(rootU, rootV); return False if no cycle found.

examples.input: Graph with 4 vertices and edges: [[0,1], [1,2], [2,3], [3,0]]
examples.trace: Initialize: parent = [0,1,2,3], rank = [0,0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots differ → union(0,1): rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, attach 1 to 0, parent=[0,0,2,3], rank=[1,0,0,0]. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0 (path: 1→0), find(2)=2, roots differ → union(0,2): rank[0]=1, rank[2]=0, attach 2 to 0, parent=[0,0,0,3], rank=[2,0,0,0]. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=0 (path: 2→0), find(3)=3, roots differ → union(0,3): rank[0]=2, rank[3]=0, attach 3 to 0, parent=[0,0,0,0], rank=[3,0,0,0]. Edge [3,0]: find(3)=0 (path: 3→0), find(0)=0, roots same → cycle detected, return True.

examples.input: Graph with 3 vertices and edges: [[0,1], [1,2]]
examples.trace: Initialize: parent = [0,1,2], rank = [0,0,0]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, roots differ → union(0,1): rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, attach 1 to 0, parent=[0,0,2], rank=[1,0,0]. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=0 (path: 1→0), find(2)=2, roots differ → union(0,2): rank[0]=1, rank[2]=0, attach 2 to 0, parent=[0,0,0], rank=[2,0,0]. All edges processed, no cycle found, return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union Operation with Union by Rank
ladders.desc: Implement the union function that merges two sets by attaching the root of the lower-rank tree to the higher-rank root.
ladders.explanation: The union operation is crucial for efficiently merging two disjoint sets in union-find. Given two root nodes (representing sets), it combines them into a single set. The intuition behind union by rank is to keep the tree shallow to optimize future find operations. Rank approximates the height of the tree (or an upper bound). When unioning roots rootX and rootY, we compare their ranks. If ranks differ, we attach the root with lower rank to the one with higher rank, leaving the higher rank unchanged. This prevents the tree from growing unnecessarily tall. If ranks are equal, we arbitrarily attach one root to the other and increment the rank of the new root by one (since the height increases by one). This ensures that the tree remains balanced, leading to near-constant time operations with path compression. The algorithm: if rank[rootX] > rank[rootY], set parent[rootY] = rootX; else if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY], set parent[rootX] = rootY; else (ranks equal), set parent[rootY] = rootX and increment rank[rootX] by 1. Time complexity is O(1) per union, and space is O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This union operation is called in the main loop after finding roots for each edge. Common mistakes include updating ranks incorrectly (e.g., incrementing when not needed) or modifying parents of non-root nodes. Real code pattern: def union(x, y): rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y); if rootX != rootY: if rank[rootX] > rank[rootY]: parent[rootY] = rootX; elif rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]: parent[rootX] = rootY; else: parent[rootY] = rootX; rank[rootX] += 1.

examples.input: Initial: parent = [0,1,2], rank = [0,0,0]. Union roots 0 and 1.
examples.trace: rootX = 0, rootY = 1. rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0 (equal). Attach rootY to rootX: parent[1] = 0. Increment rank[rootX]: rank[0] becomes 1. Result: parent = [0,0,2], rank = [1,0,0].

examples.input: Initial: parent = [0,0,2], rank = [1,0,0]. Union roots 0 and 2.
examples.trace: rootX = 0, rootY = 2. rank[0]=1, rank[2]=0 (rootX higher). Attach rootY to rootX: parent[2] = 0. Rank unchanged. Result: parent = [0,0,0], rank = [2,0,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Operation with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Implement the find function that locates the root of a set while compressing the path to optimize future finds.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is the heart of union-find, determining the representative (root) of a set for a given element. Path compression is an optimization that flattens the tree structure during finds, making subsequent operations faster. The intuition is that after finding the root for a node, we update all nodes along the path to point directly to the root, reducing the height of the tree. This ensures that future finds for these nodes or their neighbors become nearly O(1). The algorithm is recursive: if parent[x] != x, then set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) (recursively find the root and update parent), and return parent[x]. Alternatively, an iterative version uses a loop to find the root and then another loop to update parents. Time complexity is O(α(V)) amortized due to path compression, and space is O(1) extra (or O(log V) for recursion depth). Connection to L1: Find is used before union to get the roots of elements. Common mistakes include not updating parents correctly (e.g., returning without assignment) or infinite recursion in cycles (avoided by proper initialization). Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x].

examples.input: parent = [0,0,1,2]. Find(3).
examples.trace: find(3): parent[3]=2 != 3 → find(2). find(2): parent[2]=1 != 2 → find(1). find(1): parent[1]=0 != 1 → find(0). find(0): parent[0]=0 → return 0. Backtrack: set parent[1]=0, return 0; set parent[2]=0, return 0; set parent[3]=0, return 0. Final root is 0, parent array updated to [0,0,0,0].

examples.input: parent = [0,1,2,3]. Find(0).
examples.trace: find(0): parent[0]=0 → return 0. No compression needed as it's already root.

ladders.title: Building Block — Disjoint Set Representation with Parent and Rank Arrays
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the data structure to represent disjoint sets using arrays for parent pointers and ranks.
ladders.explanation: The disjoint set (union-find) data structure represents a collection of non-overlapping sets, where each set has a representative element (root). It is implemented using two arrays: parent and rank. The parent array stores the parent of each element; initially, each element is its own parent, indicating it is in a singleton set. The rank array stores an upper bound on the height of the tree rooted at each element, used to optimize unions. The intuition is that parent pointers form a forest of trees, where the root of a tree represents the set. Ranks help keep trees shallow by guiding how to attach trees during union operations. This representation allows efficient find and union operations. The algorithm initializes parent[i] = i and rank[i] = 0 for all i from 0 to V-1. Time complexity for initialization is O(V), and space is O(V) for the arrays. Connection to L2: This data structure is used by the find and union operations. Common mistakes include using incorrect array sizes or not initializing ranks to zero. Real code pattern: parent = [i for i in range(V)]; rank = [0] * V.

examples.input: Initialize for V=4.
examples.trace: For i=0: parent[0]=0, rank[0]=0. i=1: parent[1]=1, rank[1]=0. i=2: parent[2]=2, rank[2]=0. i=3: parent[3]=3, rank[3]=0. Result: parent = [0,1,2,3], rank = [0,0,0,0].

examples.input: After some operations, parent = [0,0,2,2], rank = [1,0,1,0]. Represent sets.
examples.trace: Element 0: parent[0]=0 (root). Element 1: parent[1]=0, so in set with root 0. Element 2: parent[2]=2 (root). Element 3: parent[3]=2, so in set with root 2. Thus, sets are {0,1} and {2,3}.

testCases.expected: sets: {0} with root 0, {1,2} with root 1, {3} with root 3
sets: {0,3} with root 0, {1} with root 1, {2} with root 2

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Updating for Parent Pointers
ladders.desc: Perform basic array operations to read and update parent pointers in the disjoint set.
ladders.explanation: This foundational operation involves manipulating arrays to track parent-child relationships in the disjoint set. The parent array is a list where the index represents a node, and the value at that index represents its parent node. Initially, each node points to itself. During operations, we read parent values to traverse up the tree and update them to change the structure (e.g., during union or path compression). The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access and update, making operations efficient. For example, to find the parent of node x, we access parent[x]. To set a new parent, we assign parent[y] = x. This is the mechanical basis for the find and union functions. Time complexity for a single read or update is O(1), and space is O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: These operations are used to implement the parent array in the disjoint set representation. Common mistakes include using out-of-bounds indices or confusing indices with values. Real code pattern: parent = [0,1,2]; read: p = parent[1] (gives 1); update: parent[1] = 0 (now parent[1] is 0).

examples.input: parent = [0,1,2]. Read parent[1], then update parent[1] to 0.
examples.trace: Read parent[1]: value at index 1 is 1. Update: assign parent[1] = 0. Now parent array becomes [0,0,2].

examples.input: parent = [0,0,2,2]. Read parent[3], then update parent[2] to 0.
examples.trace: Read parent[3]: value at index 3 is 2. Update: assign parent[2] = 0. Now parent array becomes [0,0,0,2].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sets and Connectivity in Graphs
ladders.desc: Grasp the basic idea of sets and how graph edges connect vertices into components.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces sets as collections of distinct elements and connectivity in graphs as relationships between vertices. In an undirected graph, vertices are points, and edges are lines connecting pairs of vertices. Two vertices are connected if there is a path between them (a sequence of edges). A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair is connected. The intuition for cycle detection is that adding an edge between two vertices already in the same connected component creates a cycle. For example, if vertices A and B are connected via some path, adding an edge A-B forms a loop. This relates to union-find by representing each connected component as a set; initially, each vertex is in its own set. When we process an edge, if the vertices are in the same set, a cycle exists. Otherwise, we merge their sets. This concept requires no code, just understanding. Time and space are not applicable here. Connection to L4: This understanding motivates why we use parent pointers to track sets. Common mistakes include confusing directed and undirected graphs or misunderstanding connectivity. Real-world analogy: think of friends in social networks—if two people are already friends (connected), introducing them again creates redundancy (a cycle).

examples.input: Graph with vertices {0,1,2} and edges {0-1, 1-2}. Are 0 and 2 connected?
examples.trace: Edge 0-1 connects 0 and 1. Edge 1-2 connects 1 and 2. Thus, 0 and 2 are connected through 1. Sets: initially {0},{1},{2}. After 0-1: merge to {0,1},{2}. After 1-2: merge to {0,1,2}. All vertices in one set, so connected.

examples.input: Graph with vertices {0,1,2} and edges {0-1, 0-2}. Add edge 1-2. Does this create a cycle?
examples.trace: After edges 0-1 and 0-2, vertices are connected: sets {0,1,2}. Adding edge 1-2 connects vertices already in the same set, forming a cycle 0-1-2-0.

testCases.input: Vertices {A,B}, edge A-B. Are A and B connected?
Vertices {A,B,C}, edges A-B, B-C. Are A and C connected?
Vertices {A,B,C}, edges A-B. Are B and C connected?
Vertices {A,B,C}, edges A-B, B-C, C-A. Is there a cycle?
Vertices {A,B,C,D}, edges A-B, C-D. Are A and D connected?
Vertices {A,B,C}, edges A-B. Add edge B-C. Is there a cycle?
Vertices {A,B,C}, edges A-B, B-C. Add edge C-A. Is there a cycle?
Vertices {A,B}, edge A-A. Is there a cycle?
Vertices {A,B,C,D}, edges A-B, B-C, C-D, D-A. Is there a cycle?
Vertices {A,B,C,D}, edges A-B, C-D. Add edge B-C. Is there a cycle?
